This guide helps architects and engineering leads select the right cloud platform and design system for their projects. It serves as the foundational decision-making resource before diving into specific UI tooling (see 

1-2 - Introduction & Decision Framework) or implementation strategies (see 

1-3 - Design System Architecture).

Start with 

1-1.1 - Introduction & Decision Framework to understand your decision criteria

Review cloud platform options below and in 

1-1.2 - Design System Architecture

Explore implementation patterns in 

1-1.3 - Implementation Patterns

Use the decision tree in 

1-1.4 - Decision Tree & Quick Reference

Link to 1-2.md for UI builder tooling specific to your platform choice

Link to 1-3.md for full tech stack and team structure alignment

developer​.microsoft​.com​/en​-us/

Strengths: Enterprise integration, .NET ecosystem, Blazor components, hybrid cloud support

Clippy is back! — With AI-enhanced features for modern development

Best For: Organizations with Windows/Office investments, enterprise applications

Developer Portal: 

developer.ibm.com/

Strengths: Enterprise solutions, hybrid cloud, enterprise AI

Best For: Large enterprises with complex legacy systems

Developer Portal: 

developers​.redhat​.com/

Strengths: Open source, Kubernetes-native, enterprise Linux support

Best For: Organizations prioritizing open source and container orchestration

Developer Portal: 

developer​.hashicorp​.com/

Strengths: Multi-cloud infrastructure as code, Terraform, Vault, Consul

Best For: Organizations managing multi-cloud environments and infrastructure

oracle​.com​/developer/

Tools: 

OCI Tools & Services

Strengths: Oracle database optimization, high performance, autonomous database

Best For: Organizations with existing Oracle database investments

Developer Portal: 

developers​.google​.com/

Strengths: ML/AI, BigQuery, Kubernetes (originated here), data analytics

Best For: Data-driven applications, machine learning pipelines, startups

Developer Portal: 

developer​.amazon​.com/

Strengths: Broadest service portfolio, serverless, longest track record

Best For: Complex multi-service architectures, established enterprises

Developer Portal: 

developer​.apple​.com/

Strengths: Native iOS/macOS development, ecosystem integration, privacy-first

Best For: Mobile-first applications, macOS applications

A design system bridges the gap between design and development, ensuring consistency across applications. Modern design systems are:

Cross-platform: Work across web, mobile, desktop

Design-to-code: Integrated workflows from design tools (Figma) to component libraries

Token-based: Separation of design tokens from implementation

Microsoft Azure teams commonly use 

Storybook to host CSS/Sass and design tokens while building a separate Blazor component library that consumes those styles. This hybrid approach combines the best of both worlds:

Workflow:Figma Design → Storybook (CSS/Design Tokens) ↔ Figma<>Storybook Plugin
                                              ↓
                                    Blazor Component Library
                                              ↓
                                    RCL (Razor Class Library)
                                              ↓
                                    Multiple Projects

Key Integration Tool: 

Storybook Connect Plugin allows you to link live stories directly to Figma components, creating a bidirectional design-to-code connection.

Cross-Reference: JPL’s 

Explorer 1 Design System demonstrates an alternative approach primarily built with React and TypeScript, supporting React, Vue, and Web Components rather than Blazor.

MudBlazor: Open-source, Material Design-based library. Highly customizable with built-in theming support. Popular for modern enterprise UIs.

Fluent UI: Microsoft’s official implementation of the 

Fluent Design System. Ensures consistency with modern Microsoft applications.

Radzen Blazor: 70+ free components with 

Radzen Blazor Studio, a WYSIWYG designer for visual logic management.

Blazorize: Switch between Bootstrap, Bulma, or Material Design CSS frameworks while maintaining consistent C# code.

Host Components in a 

Razor Class Library (RCL)

Enables component reuse across multiple projects

Keeps components platform-agnostic

Simplifies versioning and distribution

Use Blazing Story for Living Documentation

Integrate into your RCL

Creates a live catalog for designers and developers

Serves as single source of truth

Leverage 

RenderFragment

Create flexible, modular components

Support nested content and template hooks

Enable composition patterns

QT Designer: Cross-platform desktop UI design

TKinter Designer: Uses Figma as design frontend for Python

React-based Systems: Explore UI tooling in 

1-2 - Introduction & Decision Framework for language-specific options

For detailed decision frameworks, implementation patterns, and quick reference guides, see:

1-1.3

This document helps engineering teams make informed decisions about cloud platform selection and design system architecture. These foundational choices significantly impact:

Development velocity and team productivity

Long-term scalability and operational costs

Technology ecosystem availability

Compliance and security requirements

Integration capabilities with existing systems

Questions to ask:

What is your primary use case? (Web apps, mobile, backend services, data processing, AI/ML)

What is your timeline to market? (Startup velocity vs. enterprise stability)

What are your growth projections for the next 2-5 years?

Are there compliance requirements? (HIPAA, GDPR, SOC 2, government certifications)

What is your budget model? (CapEx vs. OpEx, predictable vs. variable costs)

Impact on Platform Choice:

Rapid growth startups → AWS, GCP (broad service portfolio, pay-as-you-go)

Enterprise stability → Azure, IBM, OCI (established support, integration services)

Data-intensive applications → GCP, AWS (analytics, ML services)

Compliance-heavy industries → AWS, Azure (certified compliance offerings)

Questions to ask:

What is your team’s existing cloud experience?

Do you have expertise in specific languages/frameworks? (Java/Spring, .NET/C#, Python, Node.js)

What DevOps and Kubernetes expertise exists?

Are you hiring? What skills are available in your job market?

Impact on Platform Choice:

.NET/C# teams → Azure, excellent Blazor support

Python/ML teams → GCP (Vertex AI, BigQueryML)

Java/microservices teams → AWS, GCP, Kubernetes-anywhere

Kubernetes experts → Any cloud (or on-premises via RedHat)

Questions to ask:

Monolithic vs. microservices architecture?

Serverless vs. containerized vs. traditional VMs?

Database requirements? (Relational, NoSQL, specialized databases)

Real-time processing needs? (Events, streams, batch)

Multi-cloud or single-cloud strategy?

Impact on Platform Choice:

Serverless-first → AWS Lambda, GCP Cloud Functions, Azure Functions

Kubernetes-native → GCP (native), AWS EKS, Azure AKS

Database-heavy → OCI (Oracle Autonomous Database), AWS RDS

Multi-cloud flexibility → HashiCorp stack (Terraform, Vault)

Questions to ask:

Do you have centralized or distributed teams?

What is the team size? (Startup: 5-20 engineers, Mid: 50-200, Enterprise: 200+)

Do you have dedicated DevOps/SRE teams?

Will you use managed services or build/operate your own infrastructure?

Impact on Platform Choice:

Small teams (<20) → Managed services, minimal ops overhead → GCP, Azure

Distributed teams → Strong community support → AWS

Dedicated DevOps → More control, build custom infrastructure → Any cloud

Hybrid teams → Balanced approach → Microsoft ecosystem

Questions to ask:

Do you need a unified design system across multiple applications?

What platforms do you target? (Web, mobile, desktop, embedded)

Do you have design-to-development workflow needs?

What technology stack for UI? (React, Vue, Blazor, native)

Impact on Design System Choice:

Multi-team consistency needed → Storybook + centralized component library

Web + mobile → Design tokens + platform-specific implementations

Enterprise CRUD apps → Blazor with MudBlazor or Fluent UI

Consumer apps → React or native mobile frameworks

START: Cloud Platform Selection
       ↓
Q1: What's your primary use case?
    ├─ Data/AI → GCP, AWS
    ├─ Enterprise integration → Azure, IBM
    ├─ High-performance DB → OCI
    └─ Broad portfolio → AWS
       ↓
Q2: What's your team's expertise?
    ├─ .NET/C# → Azure (strong choice)
    ├─ Python/ML → GCP, AWS
    ├─ Java/microservices → AWS, GCP, or Kubernetes-anywhere
    └─ Infrastructure as code → HashiCorp
       ↓
Q3: Architecture preference?
    ├─ Serverless → AWS (most mature)
    ├─ Kubernetes → GCP (native) or any + EKS/AKS
    ├─ Traditional VMs → Any cloud
    └─ Multi-cloud → HashiCorp
       ↓
Q4: Budget & compliance?
    ├─ Strict compliance → AWS, Azure
    ├─ Cost optimization → GCP
    ├─ Predictable budget → Azure, IBM
    └─ Pay-as-you-go scale → AWS
       ↓
RESULT: Recommended Platform(s)
       ↓
NEXT: Select design system (see 1-1.2)
      Then: UI tooling (1-2), Full architecture (1-3)

Platform: Microsoft Azure + .NET/BlazorDesign System: MudBlazor or Fluent UI with Razor Class LibraryInfrastructure: App Service or Kubernetes (AKS)

Why:

Azure integrates seamlessly with existing Microsoft products

Blazor enables isomorphic C# across client and server

Strong enterprise support and compliance certifications

Platform: Google Cloud PlatformDesign System: React with design tokens (Figma → Storybook)Infrastructure: Vertex AI, BigQuery, Kubernetes (GKE)

Why:

GCP originated Kubernetes and excels at orchestration

BigQuery and Vertex AI are industry-leading

Strong Python/ML ecosystem

Platform: AWS with multi-service architectureDesign System: Design tokens with platform-specific implementationsInfrastructure: Lambda, ECS, RDS, managed services

Why:

Broadest service portfolio enables rapid feature development

Mature community and third-party integrations

Pay-as-you-go scales with growth

Platform: HashiCorp stack across multiple cloudsDesign System: Language/framework-agnostic (web components)Infrastructure: Terraform + Vault + Consul

Why:

Vendor lock-in avoidance

Consistent infrastructure code across clouds

Flexible team distribution

If you’re migrating from existing infrastructure:

Assessment Phase

Catalog existing applications and dependencies

Identify compliance/regulatory requirements

Document team skill inventory

Pilot Phase

Select 1-2 representative applications

Build proof of concepts on target platform

Validate cost, performance, and operational requirements

Migration Phase

Establish migration sequence (highest ROI first)

Plan for zero/minimal downtime

Establish monitoring and rollback procedures

Optimization Phase

Refactor applications for cloud-native patterns

Optimize costs through reserved instances, auto-scaling

Implement FinOps practices
Risk: Technology sprawl, operational complexityMitigation: Establish clear ownership and governance for each service

records.content: Risk: UI inconsistency, reduced developer velocity, poor user experienceMitigation: Invest in design system early; it pays dividends across all teams

Document your answers to the key decision criteria

Identify any gaps in team expertise

A design system is a comprehensive collection of reusable components, patterns, and guidelines that enable consistent product experiences across all digital touchpoints.

Design System = Design Tokens + Component Library + Documentation + Design Tools

Design Tokens

The fundamental design decisions (colors, typography, spacing, shadows)

Single source of truth for visual consistency

Stored in formats like JSON, YAML, or design tool exports

Examples: color.primary = "#0066CC", spacing.unit = "8px"

Component Library

Reusable, well-tested UI components

Built in the technology stack of your choice (React, Vue, Blazor, Web Components)

Documented with clear usage guidelines

Versioned and distributed as packages

Design Documentation

Storybook, Zeroheight, or custom documentation

Communicates when, how, and why to use each component

“Living documentation” that stays in sync with code

Design Tools Integration

Figma, Sketch, Adobe XD with design tokens plugins

Bidirectional sync between design and code

Figma components mapped to code components
Recommended for: Enterprise applications, internal tools, .NET teams

Figma Design Tokens
      ↓
Storybook (CSS/Sass + Design Tokens)
      ↓ (Figma <> Storybook Plugin)
Blazor Component Library
      ↓
Razor Class Library (RCL)
      ↓
Multiple Applications

Layer

Technology

Purpose

Design Tokens

Figma + Token Studio plugin

Central design decisions

Design in Figma with tokens defined

Use Token Studio plugin to export design tokens as JSON

Synchronization

Figma <> Storybook plugin keeps designs and implementations aligned

Component changes in Storybook automatically available in Figma

Designers and developers stay synchronized

MudBlazor

Material Design foundation

30+ components out-of-the-box

Extensive customization via CSS variables

Active community and regular updates

Free and open-source

Fluent UI Blazor

Microsoft’s official design system implementation

Ensures consistency with Microsoft Office/Teams

Enterprise-grade components

Deep integration with Azure services

Best for teams already in Microsoft ecosystem

Radzen Blazor

70+ components with professional design

Radzen Blazor Studio visual designer

Drag-and-drop page builder

WYSIWYG interface reduces code writing

Good for rapid prototyping

Blazorize

Framework-agnostic approach

Switch between Bootstrap, Bulma, Material without changing C# code

Ideal for multi-tenant applications with different themes

Reduces vendor lock-in

Step 1: Establish Component DistributionHost Components in Razor Class Library (RCL)
├─ Enables reuse across multiple projects
├─ Simplifies component versioning
├─ Maintains platform independence
└─ Facilitates team distribution

Step 2: Create Living DocumentationImplement Blazing Story
├─ Live component catalog
├─ Single source of truth
├─ Shared reference for designers and developers
└─ Reduced onboarding time for new team members

Step 3: Enable Flexible CompositionUse RenderFragment patterns
├─ Create flexible, modular components
├─ Support nested content
├─ Enable template hooks and slots
└─ Reduce component proliferation
Recommended for: Startups, web-first applications, cross-platform needs

    ↓

    ↓

    ↓ (Same tokens/design)
Mobile Apps (React Native, Flutter, native)

Design tokens exported from Figma (JSON format)

Tokens committed to repository

Component Development

Consume design tokens programmatically

Application Integration

Applications install component library from npm

Automatically get latest tokens and components

No code changes needed for design updates

records.content:
JPL’s 

Explorer 1 Design System

Built with React and TypeScript

Supports React, Vue, and Web Components

Demonstrates cross-framework compatibility

Production-grade design system at scale

Storybook <> Figma Plugin

Bidirectional synchronization

Figma components link to Storybook stories

Designers can see implementation immediately

Developers can update components and reflect in Figma

Design Token Tools

Token Studio: Visual token editor in Figma

Specify: Design token management

Panda CSS: Styled system with design tokens

Specialized Designers

QT Designer: Cross-platform desktop UI

TKinter Designer: Uses Figma as frontend for Python

Components created project-by-project

Inconsistent patterns across applications

High maintenance burden

Time to market: Fast initially, slows down over time

Components centralized in a repository

Basic documentation and usage guidelines

Manual versioning and distribution

Time to market: Moderate consistency improvement

Design tokens defined and centralized

Storybook or equivalent documentation

Component library published to package manager

Design-to-code workflow established

Time to market: Significantly improved, consistent UX

Design token automation (Figma → Code)

Continuous component testing (visual regression)

Automated versioning and releases

Bidirectional sync between design and code

Time to market: Maximum velocity, zero friction

AI-assisted component generation

Predictive pattern suggestions

Automated accessibility compliance

Performance optimization recommendations

Time to market: Futuristic, still emerging

Design Tokens

Owned by design team

Reviewed by design system lead

Changes through formal process

Components

Developed by shared platform team or designated contributors

Reviewed by tech lead + designer

Semantic versioning (MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH)

Documentation

Maintained by product team

Updated with each component change

Reviewed for clarity and accuracy

1.0.0 (MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH)
│
├─ MAJOR: Breaking changes (component API changes)
├─ MINOR: New components or non-breaking features
└─ PATCH: Bug fixes and documentation updates

Release Cadence

Patch releases: As needed (bug fixes)

Minor releases: Monthly or quarterly (new components)

Major releases: Annual or as needed (strategic changes)

Quarterly reviews: Audit unused components

Dependency updates: Keep libraries current

Performance monitoring: Track bundle size and load times

Accessibility audits: Ensure WCAG 2.1 AA compliance

% of applications using design system components

Reduction in custom component creation

Component reuse rate (how many projects use each component)

Time to component availability (design → production)

Reduction in code review cycles (due to consistent patterns)

New feature delivery time

Accessibility compliance (WCAG score)

Bundle size per component

Component test coverage (unit + visual regression)

Incident reduction (UI-related bugs)

Cost per screen/page (development hours)

Reuse ROI (savings from not building duplicate components)

Design system maintenance cost vs. savings

Choose your design system pattern (Blazor, React, Web Components)

Identify design tokens relevant to your domain

Select component library based on your platform choice

Establish governance (ownership, versioning, contribution guidelines)

1-1.3 - Implementation Patterns for platform-specific tech stacks

Reference 

1-2 - Introduction & Decision Framework for UI builders to accelerate component creation

This document provides recommended tech stack patterns and architectural approaches for different use cases, connecting platform choices to implementation strategies.

Each pattern includes:

Platform choice (cloud + infrastructure)

Design system strategy (UI/component approach)

Tech stack (languages, frameworks, databases)

Architecture pattern (monolith, microservices, serverless)

Team structure (roles and responsibilities)

Link to 

1-3 - Design System Architecture for operational details
Internal tools, line-of-business applications, enterprise dashboards for established organizations.

☁️  Cloud: Microsoft Azure
🏗️  Compute: App Service or Kubernetes (AKS)
💾 Database: Azure SQL Database or Cosmos DB
🔐 Auth: Azure AD / Microsoft Entra
⚙️  IaC: Terraform or Azure Resource Manager

Single deployment unit during growth phase

Shared database initially

Clear API boundaries between Blazor frontend and backend

Lead Engineer: API Gateway + microservice patterns

2-3 Backend Developers: C#/ASP.NET Core

├─ EventBridge (event routing)
├─ SQS (job queues)
├─ SNS (pub/sub)
└─ Kinesis (streaming)

DevOps
├─ GitHub Actions or CodePipeline
├─ Docker + ECR
├─ ECS/Fargate for containers
├─ Lambda for serverless functions
└─ CloudWatch for monitoring

API Gateway
    ├─ Lambda (serverless compute)
    ├─ ECS/Fargate (containerized services)
    └─ DynamoDB + RDS
            ↓
React Frontend (web)
Event-Driven Services
    ├─ EventBridge (event routing)
    ├─ SQS (async processing)
    └─ SNS (notifications)
Mobile App (React Native)

hierarchy.lvl4: Example: Node.js API with AWS Lambda
Example: Node.js API with AWS Lambda

records.content: ✅ Broadest service portfolio (solutions for any problem)✅ Mature ecosystem and extensive documentation✅ Pay-as-you-go scaling (perfect for startups)✅ Easy onboarding (large community)✅ Flexible architecture options
⚠️  Risk of using too many AWS services (complexity)⚠️  Cost can increase unpredictably without governance⚠️  Requires strong DevOps discipline⚠️  AWS-specific knowledge required

Lead Engineer - Backend: API design, system architecture

2-3 Backend Developers: Node.js/Python services

2 Frontend Developers: React + React Native

1 DevOps/SRE: AWS services, CI/CD, monitoring
Organizations with existing infrastructure, multi-cloud requirements, infrastructure-as-code emphasis.

☁️  Cloud: AWS + GCP + Azure (multi-cloud)
🏗️  Orchestration: Terraform + Vault + Consul
💾 Database: Managed databases on each cloud
⚙️  IaC: Terraform (infrastructure as code)
🔐 Secrets: Vault (centralized secrets management)
🔍 Discovery: Consul (service discovery, health checks)

Infrastructure
├─ Terraform (IaC, multi-cloud)
├─ Ansible (configuration management)
├─ Vault (secrets management)
└─ Consul (service discovery)

Frontend
├─ Framework-agnostic
├─ Web Components (portable)
└─ Design tokens (platform-agnostic)

Backend
├─ Language-agnostic (any language)
├─ gRPC preferred (efficient, polyglot)
├─ REST fallback for simplicity
└─ Service mesh optional (Istio, Linkerd)

Data
├─ Cloud-native managed services preferred
├─ Multi-cloud vendors for failover
└─ Backup and disaster recovery critical

DevOps
├─ Terraform state management (remote backend)
├─ Policy as Code (Sentinel, OPA)
├─ Multi-cloud CI/CD (Spacelift, Terraform Cloud)
└─ Federated monitoring (cross-cloud)

Terraform Modules (reusable across clouds)
    ├─ AWS modules
    ├─ GCP modules
    └─ Azure modules
            ↓
Vault (centralized secrets)
    ↓
Consul (service discovery + health)
    ↓
Microservices (any language, any cloud)
    ├─ AWS region A
    ├─ GCP region B
    └─ Azure region C
✅ Vendor lock-in avoidance✅ Disaster recovery through multi-cloud redundancy✅ Infrastructure as code at scale✅ Consistent tooling across clouds✅ Compliance through policy enforcement

⚠️  Significant operational complexity⚠️  Requires expert-level infrastructure knowledge⚠️  Monitoring and debugging across clouds is difficult⚠️  Cost management complexity increases⚠️  Smaller community compared to single-cloud patterns

Lead Engineer: Infrastructure strategy, Terraform architecture

2-3 DevOps/SRE Engineers: Terraform, Vault, Consul expertise

Backend Developers: Service implementation (can be language-diverse)

Frontend Developers: Web Components, design tokens

Phase 1: Strangler Pattern
├─ Build new services alongside monolith
├─ Gradually route traffic to new services
└─ Retire monolith incrementally

Phase 2: Data Migration
├─ CQRS pattern for read/write separation
├─ Event sourcing for audit trail
└─ Dual-write during migration

Phase 3: Operational Maturity
├─ Service mesh implementation (Istio/Linkerd)
├─ Observability (distributed tracing)
└─ Policy enforcement (network, security)

Identify your use case from patterns above

Review tech stack for your pattern

Reference 

1-3 - Design System Architecture for operational details (monitoring, CI/CD, team structure)

Check 

1-2 - Introduction & Decision Framework for UI builder tools specific to your frontend choice

Plan pilot project to validate pattern in your organization

START
  │
  ├─→ What's your primary use case?
  │     │
  │     ├─ AI/ML, Data Analytics, Large Scale → GCP ⭐ or AWS
  │     ├─ Enterprise Integration, .NET Apps → Azure ⭐
  │     ├─ Complex Multi-service → AWS ⭐
  │     ├─ High-Performance Database → OCI ⭐
  │     ├─ Startup MVP → AWS or GCP ⭐
  │     ├─ Large Enterprise with Legacy Systems → IBM ⭐
  │     ├─ Open Source Focus → RedHat
  │     ├─ Infrastructure-as-Code Centric → HashiCorp
  │     └─ Apple Ecosystem → Apple Developer
  │
  ├─→ What's your team's expertise?
  │     │
  │     ├─ .NET/C# majority → Azure strongly recommended
  │     ├─ Python/ML specialists → GCP
  │     ├─ JavaScript/Node.js → AWS or GCP
  │     ├─ Java/Spring Boot → AWS, GCP, or any with Kubernetes
  │     ├─ Generic/Mixed → AWS (broadest options)
  │     └─ DevOps/Infrastructure → HashiCorp or any with strong IaC
  │
  ├─→ Budget constraints?
  │     │
  │     ├─ Highly cost-sensitive → GCP (good pricing) or AWS Free Tier
  │     ├─ Predictable budget needed → Azure (often cheaper for enterprises)
  │     ├─ Pay-as-you-go scaling → AWS or GCP
  │     └─ Unlimited → All viable
  │
  ├─→ Compliance/Regulatory requirements?
  │     │
  │     ├─ HIPAA → AWS, Azure, GCP (all certified)
  │     ├─ GDPR strict → AWS, Azure (mature GDPR services)
  │     ├─ Government (FedRAMP) → AWS GovCloud, Azure Government, GCP
  │     ├─ Enterprise compliance + legacy systems → IBM, AWS, Azure
  │     ├─ SOC 2 → All major clouds
  │     └─ None → Any cloud fine
  │
  └─→ RESULT: Recommended Platform(s)
        │
        ├─ Primary Choice: [Azure/AWS/GCP/OCI]
        ├─ Secondary Choice: [Alternative]
        └─→ Next: Review [1-1.3 - Implementation Patterns](1-1.3.md)

START
  │
  ├─→ What's your primary frontend platform?

  │     │   (See 1-1.2)
  │     │

  │     │   (See 1-1.2)
  │     │

  │     │   (See 1-1.2)

  │     │   (See 1-2.md)

  │
  ├─→ Do you need design-to-code workflow?
  │     │
  │     ├─ YES, Figma-to-Storybook → Install Figma<>Storybook plugin
  │     ├─ YES, design tokens → Use Token Studio or Specify
  │     └─ NO, manual updates → Storybook documentation only
  │
  ├─→ How many teams will use this system?
  │     │
  │     ├─ Single team → Start simple, grow later
  │     ├─ 2-5 teams → Formalized library with docs
  │     └─ 5+ teams → Governance needed, dedicated platform team
  │
  └─→ RESULT: Design System Pattern

        ├─ Tools: [Storybook, Figma, design tokens]
        └─→ Next: Implement following [1-1.2](1-1.2.md)

  • Strong enterprise compliance

hierarchy.lvl4: Scenario 2: Startup with Data Science Requirements
Scenario 2: Startup with Data Science Requirements

records.content:
✅ RECOMMENDED: Google Cloud Platform
  • Vertex AI for ML
  • BigQuery for analytics
  • Kubernetes originated here
  • Strong Python ecosystem
  
⚠️  BACKUP: AWS
  • When team has AWS expertise
  • SageMaker for ML
  
❌ NOT RECOMMENDED: Azure, OCI
  • Less specialized for data science

✅ RECOMMENDED: AWS or Azure
  • HIPAA, SOC 2, FedRAMP certified
  • Strong compliance tooling
  • Mature security offerings
  
⚠️  BACKUP: GCP
  • Certified for compliance
  • Smaller compliance team
  
❌ NOT RECOMMENDED: OCI, RedHat
  • Limited compliance certifications
  • Overkill for compliance focus

✅ RECOMMENDED: AWS
  • Mature edge locations worldwide
  • Strong API Gateway
  • Route 53 for global DNS
  • CloudFront CDN
  
⚠️  BACKUP: GCP
  • Good global presence
  • Cloud CDN excellent
  
❌ NOT RECOMMENDED: Azure
  • Fewer global regions historically

✅ RECOMMENDED: Oracle Cloud (OCI)
  • Autonomous Database optimized
  • Oracle DB performance
  • Migration services from Oracle
  
⚠️  BACKUP: AWS RDS
  • When avoiding Oracle licensing
  • PostgreSQL/MySQL preferred
  
❌ NOT RECOMMENDED: GCP, Azure (for Oracle workloads)
  • Less optimized for Oracle

hierarchy.lvl4: Scenario 6: Large Enterprise with Existing IBM Infrastructure
Scenario 6: Large Enterprise with Existing IBM Infrastructure

records.content:
✅ RECOMMENDED: IBM Cloud
  • Seamless Db2 and legacy system integration
  • Enterprise support and SLAs
  • Strong hybrid cloud capabilities
  • Direct path for AIX, z/OS, Power Systems workloads
  
⚠️  BACKUP: Azure, AWS
  • When modernizing away from IBM stack
  • Broader ecosystem of services
  
❌ NOT RECOMMENDED: GCP, OCI
  • Limited IBM ecosystem integration
  • Requires complete platform migration

Enable auto-scaling to match actual demand

Use managed services instead of self-hosted

| - [Qt Designer]

qt​-designer​-footnote

[qt-designer-footnote]: 

https://​doc​.qt​.io​/qt​-6​/qtdesigner​-manual​.html “Industry standard for creating complex, professional PyQt or PySide applications. Creates UI files that are loaded into Python applications...” | 

NiceGUI | 

PyUiBuilder | 

PAGE | 

Anvil |

PySimpleGUI: Highly recommended for beginners. It wraps Tkinter, Qt, and wxPython, allowing you to define layouts as simple lists of widgets, which dramatically speeds up development.

NiceGUI: A popular choice for rapid prototyping that lets you create browser-based interfaces (buttons, 3D scenes, plots) directly in Python code without needing HTML or JavaScript knowledge.

Qt Designer(PyQt5/PySide6): The industry standard for creating complex, professional PyQt or PySide applications.

Creates UI files that are loaded into Python applications.

The industry standard for PyQt and PySide. It allows you to drag widgets onto a canvas and saves the design as an .ui XML file, which can be converted to Python code or loaded directly.

It is a professional-grade tool that handles complex layouts and multi-window applications efficiently.

PyUiBuilder: A framework-agnostic, web-based drag-and-drop tool. It allows you to visually design interfaces and generate editable Python code for standard Tkinter, CustomTkinter, and other libraries.

Multi-framework	Web-based Visual	Agnostic design and rapid export

A modern, web-based drag-and-drop tool that is framework-agnostic

It supports multiple libraries like Tkinter and CustomTkinter with planned support for Kivy and PyQt, allowing you to export designs directly into Python code.

Tkinter Designer: Uses Figma as the design frontend. You design your interface in Figma, and the tool uses the Figma API to convert the design into functional Tkinter code and assets.

PyVisual: A new, user-friendly drag-and-drop builder for Python.

Pygubu: Visual designer for Tkinter, supporting drag-and-drop layout.

A Rapid Application Development (RAD) tool specifically for Tkinter. It allows you to create UI definition files (XML) that can be loaded directly into your Python scripts. files to define interfaces, similar to how Glade works for GTK.

Glade/PyGObject: RAD tool for GTK+ interfaces, ideal for Linux.

PAGE(Python Automatic GUI Generator): A drag-and-drop generator specifically for Tkinter, Python’s standard GUI library.

One of the most established tools for Tkinter. It is a cross-platform generator that resembles Visual Basic and supports both standard Tk and ttk (themed) widgets.

A mature, cross-platform tool specifically for Tkinter. It resembles older Visual Basic style environments and allows for easy placement of Tk and Ttk widgets.

It resembles older Visual Basic environments and is highly effective for building standard Windows-style interfaces.

Gooey: Automatically turns command-line programs into GUI applications with almost no extra code.

Anvil: A web-based drag-and-drop builder for creating full-stack web apps entirely in Python including the UI and the backend.

Web apps with zero frontend code

Handles hosting and deployment automatically.

MD Python Designer: A commercial-grade designer suite that supports multiple libraries including Tkinter, Kivy, Flet, and PySide2.

CustomTkinter Builder: A specialized drag-and-drop editor designed for the CustomTkinter library, which provides a more modern, dark-mode-compatible look compared to standard Tkinter.

Tkinter GUI Designer (by LabDeck): A commercial-grade component of the MD Python designer package. It provides a specialized IDE with a visual designer that can run multiple GUI instances simultaneously and generate production-ready code.

Scene Builder: The primary visual layout tool for JavaFX, which uses FXML to keep design separate from application logic.

NetBeans GUI Builder (Matisse): A well-known built-in tool for the NetBeans IDE that simplifies Swing layout management.

WindowBuilder: A powerful Eclipse plugin that supports both Swing and SWT designers with bi-directional code generation.

Visual Studio Designer: Microsoft’s premier tool for WinForms and WPF (XAML) development.

Glade Interface Designer: The dedicated tool for designing GTK+ interfaces, often used for Linux desktop apps.

Framer / Webflow: Top-tier web design and development tools for creating interactive websites.

WaveMaker: Low-code platform for creating enterprise web applications.

App Sketcher: Creates interactive web prototypes using HTML and jQuery.

Qt Creator: A full IDE that includes a deep visual editor for C++ Qt development.

Cross-platform (C++, Python) IDE with a powerful visual editor.

wxFormBuilder: A cross-platform visual designer for wxPython.

produces C++, Python, or XML code for native-looking desktop applications.

Flet: A framework that lets you build interactive Flutter-based apps in Python, often used with simple layout concepts.

Lazarus: A professional open-source visual IDE for Rapid Application Development (RAD) using Free Pascal.

Microsoft Visual Studio: Standard for Windows application development (WPF, WinForms, UWP).

Embedded Wizard: Optimized for creating GUIs on embedded systems.

JUCE: Popular for audio and multimedia application development.

Godot Engine: While a game engine, it is highly capable of creating 2D/3D GUIs and app front-ends.

Xcode: Official IDE for macOS and iOS app development.

Lazarus IDE: Open-source Free Pascal RAD IDE, similar to Delphi.

Xojo: Cross-platform tool for desktop, web, and mobile app creation.

This section compares major deployment platforms across key criteria: best use cases, key advantages, free tier offerings, and database support. Choose based on your application requirements, team expertise, and operational complexity tolerance.

Platform

Best For

Key Advantages

Free Tier

Database Support

Production workloads, complex architectures, high availability

Broadest service ecosystem, mature & battle-tested, global infrastructure (33+ regions), cost optimization tools, reserved instances

PostgreSQL (12-17), MySQL (5.7-8.0), MariaDB, Oracle, SQL Server, DynamoDB, Aurora

Google Cloud Run

Containerized microservices, APIs, event-driven apps

Auto-scaling to zero, 100ms cold starts, container-native, integrated CI/CD, per-100ms billing

2M free requests/month + $0.40 per M requests

Cloud SQL (MySQL, PostgreSQL, SQL Server), Firestore, BigTable, Cloud Spanner

Azure App Service

Native .NET runtime, Visual Studio integration, built-in CI/CD, easy scaling

12-month free tier ($15 compute + databases), then pay-as-you-go

SQL Database, MySQL, PostgreSQL, Cosmos DB, Azure Data Lake

Heroku

Rapid prototyping, small to medium MVPs, learning/demos

Simplest git-based deployments, minimal DevOps knowledge, built-in CI/CD, add-on marketplace

$7/mo basic dyno (single dyno only)

50% cheaper than Heroku, intuitive UI, excellent docs, predictable pricing

Platform.sh

PythonAnywhere

Python web apps, beginners, rapid development

Python-native platform, simplest setup, no DevOps, web-based IDE

MySQL, SQLite (limited)

In modern distributed database architectures, the control plane is the administrative layer that manages the lifecycle, configuration, and health of the database instances, while the data plane handles the actual storage and retrieval of user data.

Go

Kafka,

For Redis, the control plane is typically implemented through specialized management services or Kubernetes operators:

Redis Data Integration (RDI): Features a control plane consisting of an API server for observation, an operator process to manage data plane pods, and a metrics exporter.

Redis Connect: Explicitly segregates Job Management (control plane) from Job Execution (data plane). The control plane handles job orchestration, metrics reporting, and cluster management through a REST API and CLI.

Kubernetes Operators: Solutions like the 

Kuaishou solution with KubeBlocks use a centralized control plane for cross-cluster orchestration, resource distribution, and status aggregation across multiple member clusters.

Redis Operator (CloudNativePG style): Uses a Controller Manager in the control plane to reconcile cluster resources while the Instance Manager handles local processes.

MongoDB management, especially in cloud-native or Kubernetes environments, relies on a centralized management layer:

MongoDB Atlas: Provides a full 

cloud-based developer data platform where the control plane manages security features, cluster scaling, and automated backups.

Atlas Security: The control plane manages inbound IP access lists and network security configurations to ensure only authorized traffic reaches the data clusters.

MongoDB Kubernetes Operator: Separates management logic from the database pods, allowing the control plane to manage replica sets and coordinate sharded cluster configurations across multiple Kubernetes clusters.

In hybrid architectures, third-party platforms often use both databases to support their own control and data planes:

API Management (e.g., Tyk): The 

Tyk Gateway utilizes Redis for real-time data plane operations (like rate limiting) and MongoDB or PostgreSQL for the control plane to store persistent API definitions and analytics.

Development Frameworks (e.g., LangSmith): The 

LangSmith data plane can use a custom Redis instance for its own internal control/data operations and MongoDB as an alternative backend for checkpoint storage.

The High-Performamce Scientist

Primary Use: Numerical & Scientific

Performance: High (near C++)

Ease of Use: High

Ecosystem: Growing

Best for: Complex scientific simulations, differential equations, and niche high-performance research.

Pros: Solves the “two-language problem” - you can write high-level code that runs at the speed of C++ without needing a seperate compiled language.

Cons: “Time-to-first-plot” lag caused by JIT compilation can be frustrating during interactive use.

The All-Rounder

Primary Use: General-purpose & AI

Performance: Moderate (slow core)

Ease of Use: Extreme

Ecosystem: Huge

Best for: Rapid prototyping, web development, and standard AI/ML using 

TensorFlow or 

Pytorch

Pros: It is arguably the easiest to learn with the largest community and job market presence

Cons: It is inherently slow for raw numerical loops; it relies on C/C++ backends for speed (like 

NumPy)

The Systems Powerhouse

Primary Use: Systems & High-Perf

Performance: Extreme (Native)

Ease of Use: Low (Steep curve)

Ecosystem: Mature (Software)

Best for: Operating systems, game engines, and performance-critical backends for languages like Python.

Pros: Offers total control over memory management and hardware, making it the gold standard for execution speed.

Cons: Difficult to learn due to manual memory management and complex syntax.

The Statistician’s Choice

Primary Use: Statistics & Analytics

Performance: Moderate (Stats-focused)

Ease of Use: Moderate

Ecosystem: Rich (Statistics)

Best for: Pure Statistical analysis, bioinformatics, and publication-quality data visualization using 

ggplot2.

Pros: Built specifically for statistics; handling missing data and complex data frames is often more intuitive than in Python.

Cons: Not ideal for general software engineering or big data processing compared to Python or Julia.
